sections.relevance_signal: Recurrent abdominal pain, bloating, cramping, diarrhoea, constipation, or alternating bowel habits — often diagnosed after other causes have been ruled out
sections.plain_summary: IBS (irritable bowel syndrome) is a diagnosis given to people with ongoing gut symptoms — pain, bloating, diarrhoea, constipation, or a mix — when standard tests come back normal. It is one of the most common diagnoses in gastroenterology, yet it is also one of the most misunderstood. For many people, 'IBS' is less a complete explanation and more a starting point: a label that says 'your gut is misbehaving and we haven't found a structural cause yet.' The honest reality is that IBS is almost certainly not a single condition. It is a symptom cluster that can be driven by many different underlying mechanisms — including carbohydrate malabsorption (such as lactase deficiency, fructose malabsorption, or sucrase-isomaltase deficiency), bacterial imbalances in the small intestine (hydrogen SIBO, methane SIBO, or hydrogen sulphide SIBO), gut dysbiosis, infections such as H. pylori or parasites, food sensitivities including non-coeliac gluten sensitivity or histamine intolerance, immune-mediated conditions like mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS), structural issues like bile acid malabsorption or exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, and nervous system factors like central sensitisation, anxiety, depression, or unresolved trauma. Many people with an IBS label carry one or more of these underlying drivers that have simply not been investigated. Self-directed research — tracking food, symptoms, and patterns — is a legitimate and often essential part of finding answers, especially when mainstream care has focused on managing symptoms rather than finding causes.
sections.detailed_summary:
IBS is defined by the Rome IV criteria as recurrent abdominal pain at least one day per week, associated with changes in stool frequency or form, over a period of at least three months. It is subtyped by predominant bowel habit: IBS-D (diarrhoea-predominant), IBS-C (constipation-predominant), IBS-M (mixed), and IBS-U (unclassified). The diagnosis is clinical — there is no definitive biomarker — and is typically made after excluding structural disease, coeliac disease, and inflammatory bowel disease.

The core problem with the IBS label is that it describes what is happening (dysregulated bowel function with pain) but not why. Research over the past two decades has identified a range of plausible mechanisms that can produce an IBS-like picture:

**Carbohydrate malabsorption** is one of the most common and underdiagnosed drivers. Lactase deficiency causes symptoms when lactose from dairy reaches the colon undigested. Fructose malabsorption means excess fructose is fermented by colonic bacteria, generating gas and drawing water into the bowel. Sucrase-isomaltase deficiency — still rarely tested — impairs digestion of sucrose and starch. Sorbitol and polyol intolerance is often bundled under the FODMAP framework, which attempts to address these collectively through dietary restriction.

**Microbial imbalances** are increasingly recognised as relevant. Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO), including hydrogen SIBO, methane SIBO (now sometimes called intestinal methanogen overgrowth, or IMO), and hydrogen sulphide SIBO, can each produce distinct symptom patterns. Gut dysbiosis — disrupted composition of the intestinal microbiome — is documented in IBS populations though causation remains complex. Fungal overgrowth (SIFO) and parasitic infections are less discussed but can present identically to IBS.

**Immune and inflammatory mechanisms** are an active research area. Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS) can cause widespread gut hypersensitivity, and elevated mucosal mast cells have been found in IBS tissue samples. Histamine intolerance — where histamine accumulates due to impaired breakdown — can mimic or worsen IBS. Eosinophilic GI disorders are a rarer but important differential. Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS) may drive symptoms in a subset of people despite normal coeliac serology.

**Digestive insufficiency** conditions are sometimes missed. Bile acid malabsorption (BAM) is a documented cause of chronic watery diarrhoea that is often labelled IBS-D; studies suggest it may account for up to a third of IBS-D cases. Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI) reduces enzyme output, causing maldigestion and IBS-like symptoms.

**Nervous system and brain-gut axis factors** are central to IBS pathophysiology. Central sensitisation and visceral hypersensitivity mean the gut's pain signalling is amplified — normal bowel sensations register as pain. This is a real physiological change, not psychological weakness. Anxiety disorder, depression, and trauma/PTSD have well-documented bidirectional relationships with gut function via the vagus nerve, HPA axis, and autonomic nervous system. For some people, addressing nervous system regulation is the primary lever for improvement.

**Connective tissue and systemic conditions** overlap meaningfully with IBS. Hypermobile EDS (hEDS) and hypermobility spectrum disorder are associated with gut dysmotility and are significantly overrepresented in IBS populations. POTS and dysautonomia affect gut motility and blood flow. Endometriosis — particularly intestinal endometriosis — is frequently misdiagnosed as IBS for years in people who menstruate. Hormonal and menstrual cycle effects on gut motility are real and often unaddressed. Pelvic floor dysfunction can directly impair defecation and cause significant pain.

**Other conditions** that are often confused with IBS or found alongside it include microscopic colitis (a cause of chronic watery diarrhoea that is only visible on biopsy), IBD in remission (which can produce IBS-like symptoms even without active inflammation), gastroparesis, functional dyspepsia, thyroid dysfunction, and surgical adhesions.

The practical takeaway is that a thorough investigation of IBS should not stop at ruling out coeliac disease and IBD. Many of the conditions above have specific tests — breath tests for SIBO and carbohydrate malabsorption, SeHCAT or blood FGF19 for BAM, faecal elastase for EPI, stool testing for parasites and dysbiosis — and can often be addressed once identified. Finding a practitioner who investigates causes rather than only managing symptoms is often a key step. This may mean seeking out a FODMAP-trained dietitian, a functional medicine doctor, a specialist in SIBO, or a gynaecologist with endometriosis expertise, depending on the symptom pattern.

prevalence.summary: Affects roughly 1 in 10 people worldwide, making it one of the most common functional gastrointestinal diagnoses
prevalence.estimate: 5–15% of the global population, with most estimates clustering around 10–11%
prevalence.notes: Prevalence varies considerably by diagnostic criteria used (Rome III vs Rome IV) and geography. Rome IV criteria yield lower estimates (around 4–5%) than Rome III. IBS is approximately twice as common in women as in men in Western populations, though this gap is less marked in South Asian studies. A large proportion of those affected — estimated at 40–75% — never seek medical care, meaning population figures likely undercount the true burden. IBS accounts for a substantial proportion of gastroenterology outpatient consultations globally.

patterns.label: IBS-D — diarrhea-predominant
patterns.description: Loose or watery stools are the dominant feature, often urgent and occurring after meals. Cramping and bloating are common, and symptoms may be triggered by stress, food, or both.

patterns.label: IBS-C — constipation-predominant
patterns.description: Hard, infrequent, or difficult-to-pass stools dominate, often accompanied by abdominal pain and bloating that may partially improve after a bowel movement.

patterns.label: IBS-M — mixed bowel habits
patterns.description: Both diarrhea and constipation occur in the same person, sometimes alternating within days or even hours. This pattern can be particularly difficult to manage with a single treatment approach.

patterns.label: Bloating and gas dominant
patterns.description: Significant abdominal distension, visible bloating, and excessive gas are the primary complaints, with bowel habit changes being secondary or variable.

patterns.label: Pain-dominant / visceral hypersensitivity pattern
patterns.description: Abdominal pain is the most disabling symptom, often out of proportion to visible gut changes, suggestive of heightened pain signalling in the gut-brain axis.

patterns.label: Post-infectious IBS (PI-IBS)
patterns.description: Symptoms began or dramatically worsened following an episode of gastroenteritis, food poisoning, or traveller's diarrhea, suggesting microbial or immune disruption as a trigger.

patterns.label: Symptoms beyond the gut
patterns.description: Conditions where significant symptoms appear outside the digestive system — brain fog, fatigue, skin rashes, joint pain, or widespread pain — often alongside or instead of classic gut symptoms. If gut issues come with unexplained systemic symptoms, investigating these drivers is especially worthwhile.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: For many people, 'IBS' is not a final diagnosis — it is a placeholder that means no one has yet identified the underlying cause. Accepting a management-only approach often means decades of symptom control without resolution.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Being told 'IBS' can feel like an answer after a frightening period of uncertainty and medical testing. Questioning it means accepting that more work needs to be done, often by the patient themselves, and that many clinicians will not lead that investigation.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who push past the label — methodically investigating SIBO, carbohydrate malabsorption, bile acid malabsorption, microbiome composition, and nervous system factors — find treatable causes in a substantial proportion of cases. Systematic investigation, not passive management, is what leads to real answers.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The low-FODMAP diet can be a powerful diagnostic and symptomatic tool, but it is not a cure and should not become a permanent, ever-restricting way of eating. Many people end up nutritionally depleted and socially isolated from progressively cutting more foods.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The low-FODMAP diet provides real symptom relief for many people, which makes it feel like the right long-term answer. The fact that a food causes symptoms does not mean that food is inherently the problem — the underlying microbiome state, enzyme deficiency, or gut motility issue is.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who use a FODMAP elimination as a temporary diagnostic tool — working with a trained dietitian to systematically reintroduce foods and identify their specific triggers — end up with a much wider and more sustainable diet than those who remain on full restriction indefinitely. The goal is to understand the specific intolerance mechanism, not to avoid everything indefinitely.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Stress, trauma, and nervous system dysregulation are not excuses or 'all in the head' — they produce measurable physiological changes in gut permeability, motility, and pain signalling. Ignoring the brain-gut axis means treating only part of the problem.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Many people — understandably — have been told their symptoms are psychological when in fact they have real, uninvestigated physical drivers. Being told to consider stress or trauma can feel like a dismissal rather than a legitimate medical direction.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who address both physical drivers (microbial, dietary, structural) and nervous system factors (through gut-directed hypnotherapy, somatic therapy, trauma-informed psychotherapy, or paced breathing practices) typically achieve more complete and durable improvement than those who pursue either track alone.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Post-infectious IBS — triggered by food poisoning or gastroenteritis — has a documented physiological cause: damage to interstitial cells of Cajal and anti-vinculin antibodies that impair gut motility. Many people in this situation have been told the infection is long gone and their ongoing symptoms are functional, when a plausible structural mechanism exists.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: This mechanism is not yet routinely tested in mainstream gastroenterology. Blood tests for anti-CdtB and anti-vinculin antibodies exist but are not standard care. Patients are often unaware this connection exists.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People with IBS that started after a clear gastroenteritis event who test positive for anti-vinculin antibodies have a specific and plausible explanation for SIBO-driven symptoms, and targeted treatment of SIBO in this context — particularly with rifaximin — has evidence behind it.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Endometriosis is missed for an average of 7–10 years and is routinely misdiagnosed as IBS in people who menstruate. If gut symptoms worsen during menstruation or around ovulation, IBS is not a satisfactory diagnosis until endometriosis has been properly excluded.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Most gynaecologists and gastroenterologists do not cross-refer for this overlap. Patients are given an IBS diagnosis, and the menstrual connection is noted but not acted upon. Normal pelvic ultrasound does not exclude deep infiltrating endometriosis.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who pursue assessment from a specialist endometriosis centre — including MRI and diagnostic laparoscopy when indicated — find that treating endometriosis directly can substantially reduce or resolve the bowel symptoms that had been labelled IBS.

key_levers.lever: Systematic investigation of underlying drivers rather than symptom management
key_levers.why_it_matters: IBS is a heterogeneous label. Treating underlying causes — whether SIBO, lactase deficiency, BAM, MCAS, or others — typically produces much more durable improvement than symptom-suppressing medications alone.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin by tracking symptoms against food intake, bowel patterns, menstrual cycle (if relevant), and stress levels for 2–4 weeks. Identify patterns, then pursue specific testing: breath tests for SIBO and carbohydrate malabsorption, faecal elastase for EPI, SeHCAT or FGF19 for BAM, stool tests for parasites, thyroid function, and full coeliac serology.
key_levers.timeline: Several months to complete a thorough diagnostic workup; improvement once a treatable cause is identified may begin within weeks of appropriate treatment

key_levers.lever: Structured FODMAP elimination and reintroduction — as a diagnostic tool, not a permanent diet
key_levers.why_it_matters: The low-FODMAP diet is the best-evidenced dietary intervention for IBS symptom relief, with roughly 50–75% of people experiencing significant improvement. More importantly, a structured reintroduction phase identifies which specific FODMAP subgroups are problematic, allowing a personalised and nutritionally adequate long-term diet.
key_levers.how_to_start: Work with a FODMAP-trained registered dietitian. Monash University's FODMAP app is a reliable, evidence-based guide to food content. Do not attempt the full protocol without guidance — unsupported self-restriction leads to unnecessary restriction and missed reintroduction data.
key_levers.timeline: 2–6 weeks for strict elimination phase; 8–12 weeks for full reintroduction

key_levers.lever: Gut-directed psychological therapies — particularly gut-directed hypnotherapy and CBT
key_levers.why_it_matters: Gut-directed hypnotherapy has some of the strongest and most durable evidence for IBS of any single intervention, including in people whose symptoms are not primarily anxiety-driven. It works via the gut-brain axis by reducing visceral hypersensitivity and normalising gut motor patterns — not by telling people their symptoms are imaginary.
key_levers.how_to_start: The Monash University Nerva app provides a validated gut-directed hypnotherapy programme. Alternatively, seek a therapist trained in the Manchester protocol for IBS hypnotherapy. IBS-specific CBT programmes are also available online in several countries.
key_levers.timeline: 6–12 weeks of regular practice; many people notice change within 4–6 weeks

key_levers.lever: Addressing nervous system regulation and stress physiology
key_levers.why_it_matters: Chronic sympathetic nervous system activation — whether from ongoing stress, anxiety, or unresolved trauma — directly impairs gut motility, increases intestinal permeability, and amplifies pain. Calming the autonomic nervous system is a mechanistic, not metaphorical, intervention.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin with daily diaphragmatic (slow belly) breathing — 5–6 breaths per minute for 10 minutes — which activates the vagus nerve and shifts the nervous system toward parasympathetic tone. Build toward trauma-informed therapy, somatic experiencing, or EMDR if trauma history is relevant.
key_levers.timeline: Breathing practices can reduce acute symptoms within days; sustained nervous system change requires consistent practice over weeks to months

key_levers.lever: Targeted microbiome support — addressing specific imbalances rather than generic probiotic use
key_levers.why_it_matters: Specific probiotic strains have evidence for specific IBS subtypes — for example, Lactobacillus plantarum 299v for bloating, and Bifidobacterium-predominant formulations for IBS-C. Generic probiotic supplementation without understanding the underlying microbiome state is unlikely to produce meaningful benefit and can worsen SIBO.
key_levers.how_to_start: Address SIBO before pursuing probiotics — adding organisms to an overgrown small intestine can worsen symptoms. If SIBO has been excluded or treated, choose strains with specific trial evidence for the relevant symptom subtype, guided by a knowledgeable practitioner.

key_levers.lever: Pelvic floor physiotherapy for constipation-predominant or pain-predominant IBS
key_levers.why_it_matters: Dyssynergic defecation and hypertonic pelvic floor are common, underdiagnosed contributors to constipation, incomplete evacuation, and pelvic pain. Standard gastroenterology care rarely addresses this. A trained pelvic floor physiotherapist can identify and treat it directly.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a referral to a pelvic floor physiotherapist (not a general physio) with GI or pelvic pain experience. Assessment typically involves internal examination — this should be clearly explained and fully consented. Improvement often begins within a few sessions.
key_levers.timeline: 4–12 sessions, with improvement often noticed within the first 2–4

common_mistakes: Staying on the low-FODMAP diet indefinitely without completing the reintroduction phase — this prevents identifying specific tolerances and causes unnecessary nutritional restriction
Taking probiotics during active SIBO — adding bacteria to an overgrown small intestine can worsen bloating and discomfort
Treating anxiety or depression as a cause to be addressed 'instead of' gut investigation — the relationship is bidirectional and both tracks often need to be worked simultaneously
Accepting that endometriosis has been 'ruled out' based on a normal ultrasound alone — deep infiltrating and intestinal endometriosis is frequently not visible on standard pelvic ultrasound

Pursuing every elimination diet simultaneously — eliminating gluten, dairy, FODMAPs, histamine, and salicylates at the same time makes it impossible to identify which restriction is producing benefit, and often results in severe nutritional inadequacy
Expecting a single treatment to resolve IBS completely — because multiple underlying factors often co-exist, improvement typically requires addressing several drivers in sequence or parallel
Not testing for bile acid malabsorption in diarrhoea-predominant IBS — BAM is a common and treatable cause of IBS-D that is rarely tested in standard gastroenterology workups
Confusing 'functional' (no structural abnormality found) with 'no real cause' — the absence of visible structural disease does not mean the condition is imaginary or untreatable

specialists.role: Guides the structured FODMAP elimination and reintroduction protocol — the most important dietary intervention for IBS. Also helps identify specific carbohydrate intolerances (lactose, fructose, polyols) and ensures nutritional adequacy throughout. The single most practically useful specialist for most people with IBS.
specialists.notes: Look for dietitians certified or trained by Monash University's FODMAP programme. Many gastroenterologists do not refer to dietitians by default — it may need to be requested directly.

specialists.role: Appropriate for ruling out structural disease (coeliac, IBD, microscopic colitis), performing and interpreting breath tests for SIBO and carbohydrate malabsorption, and managing complex or severe cases. Some gastroenterologists specialise in functional bowel disorders and gut-brain medicine.
specialists.notes: Mainstream gastroenterologists often focus on excluding serious disease and then managing IBS symptoms with medication. Finding one who investigates drivers — including SIBO, BAM, and EPI — is valuable but not universal.

specialists.role: Takes a root-cause investigative approach, often ordering stool microbiome analysis, comprehensive SIBO breath testing, food sensitivity panels, and hormone and thyroid investigation. More likely than mainstream gastroenterology to investigate the full range of potential IBS drivers.
specialists.notes: Quality varies widely. Seek practitioners with formal functional medicine training (e.g. IFM-certified). Some tests offered (e.g. IgG food panels) are of contested clinical utility — a good functional medicine doctor will contextualise results carefully rather than triggering excessive elimination.

specialists.role: Clinicians (often gastroenterologists or functional medicine doctors) with specific expertise in diagnosing and treating SIBO using breath testing, dietary protocols (including the specific carbohydrate diet and low-fermentation eating), and antimicrobial treatment.
specialists.notes: SIBO treatment protocols are evolving rapidly. Seek practitioners familiar with current evidence including elemental diets, rifaximin for hydrogen SIBO, and neomycin or lovastatin for methane SIBO.

specialists.role: Essential for people with constipation-predominant IBS, painful defecation, pelvic pain, or incomplete evacuation. Can assess and treat dyssynergic defecation and hypertonic pelvic floor through internal and external techniques.
specialists.notes: Must be specifically trained in pelvic floor dysfunction — not a general physiotherapist. Often dramatically underutilised in IBS care despite strong evidence.

specialists.role: Critical for people who menstruate with IBS-like gut symptoms that worsen cyclically, or with any pelvic pain. Can properly exclude deep infiltrating endometriosis through expert MRI interpretation and diagnostic laparoscopy.
specialists.notes: General gynaecologists frequently miss intestinal endometriosis. Seek a specialist endometriosis centre or accredited surgeon. Normal ultrasound does not exclude the condition.

specialists.role: Delivers gut-directed hypnotherapy (Manchester protocol) or IBS-specific CBT, both of which have strong evidence for reducing visceral hypersensitivity and gut symptoms. Particularly relevant for people with anxiety, trauma history, or symptoms that worsen markedly with stress.
specialists.notes: This is not 'being told it's all in your head' — gut-directed hypnotherapy works through demonstrable physiological mechanisms. Look for therapists with specific IBS or gut-brain training rather than general hypnotherapists.

specialists.role: Relevant if MCAS, eosinophilic GI disorders, or IgE-mediated food allergy is suspected alongside IBS symptoms. Can perform mast cell and allergy workup.
specialists.notes: MCAS remains under-recognised and some allergists may not test for it proactively. Seeking one familiar with functional mast cell disorders is helpful.

patient_communities.name: r/ibs
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community with a wide range of experience levels. Useful for peer support, sharing diagnostic journeys, and learning about less commonly investigated drivers. Quality of information varies — treat as a starting point, not a clinical source.

patient_communities.name: r/SIBO
patient_communities.notes: Focused specifically on SIBO — highly relevant for people with IBS investigating bacterial overgrowth. Often more practically detailed than general IBS communities, with discussions of breath testing, treatment protocols, and dietary approaches.

patient_communities.name: r/fodmaps
patient_communities.notes: Focused community for people working through the low-FODMAP diet. Useful for practical food questions, app guidance, and sharing reintroduction experiences. Moderated with a reasonably high standard for accuracy.

patient_communities.name: IBS Network
patient_communities.notes: UK charity providing evidence-based patient information, a self-care programme, and a peer support community. One of the more reliable patient-facing IBS resources.

patient_communities.name: IBS Patient Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Large Facebook group with active peer discussion. Useful for sharing experiences across a wide range of IBS presentations and co-conditions. Quality of advice is variable — verify medical claims independently.

patient_communities.name: Endometriosis UK forum
patient_communities.notes: Specifically relevant for people with IBS-like gut symptoms who have not been assessed for endometriosis. Provides condition information, surgeon directories, and peer support.

learning_resources.title: The IBS Solution (Monash University FODMAP resources)
learning_resources.description: The authoritative source for evidence-based FODMAP information, maintained by the research group that developed the low-FODMAP diet. Includes dietary guidance, food composition data, a certified app, and practitioner resources. Essential starting point for anyone considering the FODMAP approach.

learning_resources.title: Monash FODMAP App
learning_resources.description: The gold-standard reference for FODMAP content in foods. Allows people to check individual foods and plan meals during the elimination and reintroduction phases. Based on laboratory-tested data rather than estimates.

learning_resources.title: The Gut-Brain Axis: How the Microbiome Influences the Brain
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive scientific review of the bidirectional relationship between the gut microbiome and the brain. Provides a rigorous mechanistic foundation for understanding why stress, mood, and gut symptoms are so tightly interconnected in IBS.

learning_resources.title: A New IBS Solution
learning_resources.description: Written by one of the leading SIBO researchers, this book explains the bacterial origins of post-infectious IBS, the anti-vinculin antibody mechanism, and the evidence base for rifaximin treatment. Valuable for people whose IBS began after gastroenteritis or food poisoning.

learning_resources.title: Nerva — Gut-Directed Hypnotherapy App
learning_resources.description: A validated gut-directed hypnotherapy programme based on the evidence-backed Manchester protocol, delivered as a guided audio app. Clinical trials support its effectiveness for reducing IBS symptoms — particularly useful for people with visceral hypersensitivity or anxiety-related gut symptoms.

learning_resources.title: Gut: The Inside Story of Our Body's Most Underrated Organ
learning_resources.description: An accessible and scientifically sound introduction to gut physiology, the microbiome, and gut-brain communication. A good starting point for people who want to understand how their digestive system works before diving into IBS-specific investigation.

learning_resources.title: The SIBO Doctor podcast
learning_resources.description: A detailed practitioner and patient resource covering SIBO diagnosis, treatment protocols, breath testing interpretation, and related conditions. One of the more clinically rigorous lay-accessible sources on SIBO and its relationship to IBS.

learning_resources.title: Irritable Bowel Syndrome: Methods, Mechanisms, and Pathophysiology — global research literature
learning_resources.description: PubMed search for current peer-reviewed IBS research. Useful for people who want to access primary literature on specific mechanisms — SIBO, visceral hypersensitivity, microbiome, BAM, and more. Filter by review articles for accessible summaries.

learning_resources.title: About IBS — International Foundation for Gastrointestinal Disorders
learning_resources.description: A patient-facing resource from a respected GI advocacy organisation. Covers IBS diagnosis, subtypes, treatment options, and research updates in plain language. Good for understanding how mainstream medicine approaches IBS management.

learning_resources.title: Bile Acid Malabsorption and IBS-D — patient information
learning_resources.description: Includes accessible information on bile acid malabsorption as a cause of chronic diarrhoea frequently mislabelled as IBS-D. Useful for people with predominantly diarrhoeal IBS who have not been tested for BAM.
